methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $124M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: planqc

data.narrative:
Quantum Art is a Series A quantum computing company founded by Tal David and Nitzan Akerman that has raised $124M to date. The quantum computing sector contains 11 comparable companies (3 public, 8 private), including peers such as IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion.

Peer-median valuation analysis was not applicable due to insufficient narrow-stage comparables. The quantum computing peer set lacks adequate same-stage or adjacent-stage companies at similar valuation tiers to establish a robust median benchmark. Consequently, valuation relies on stage-progression methodology, which applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A advancement to the $124M total capital raised proxy, yielding an implied valuation of $248M.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $211M to $285M, centered on a $248M median. This range reflects ±15% sensitivity around the stage-progression result. Key uncertainties include the appropriateness of the 2.0x multiplier for quantum computing specifically—a sector with high technical and commercialization risk that may warrant different progression dynamics than software or other frontier-tech verticals—and the absence of direct peer comparables at the Series A stage, which limits calibration to broader sector composition. A more robust valuation would benefit from expanded private-company peer data at equivalent stages and articulation of quantum computing sector-specific financing patterns.
